behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode spans two boundaries — how the tool invokes git (must pass rename-detection flags like --find-renames or --diff-filter=R) and how it parses git's rename output. A Unit test with mocked git output can verify parsing logic but cannot catch a bug where the tool never requests rename detection from git, causing git to report the rename as a plain delete+add. REAL-INFRA: A real git subprocess on a real repository is needed to exercise git's similarity-based rename detection end-to-end. MOCK-HIDE: Injecting a pre-crafted rename line into mocked git output would bypass the invocation bug entirely.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a real git repository, commit a file, rename it (git mv), commit again, then invoke the tool as a subprocess. Assert: (a) the 'renamed' category count is 1, (b) the 'added' and 'deleted' category counts are both 0 for that file, and (c) the file does not appear in any other category.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The crash risk is in reading and decoding real git diff output for binary files, which contains null bytes and non-UTF-8 sequences. Git prints 'Binary files ... differ' rather than a text diff, and the tool must handle this without choking on encoding. REAL-INFRA: A real git subprocess is required because the actual byte stream git emits for binary diffs is what triggers the crash — a mock string would not reproduce the encoding edge case. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking with a clean ASCII representation of the binary diff message would bypass the real failure mode.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a real git repository, commit a binary file (e.g., a small PNG or a file with null bytes written via Python's open(..., 'wb')), then invoke the tool as a subprocess. Assert: exit code is 0, stdout is parseable, and the binary file appears with a count ≥ 1 in the appropriate change category.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: An empty git repository (post-init, pre-commit) causes standard git commands (git log, git diff HEAD, git rev-parse HEAD) to return non-zero exit codes or stderr output. The tool's failure mode is treating these error signals as fatal rather than handling them as 'nothing to report'. This can only be triggered by a real git subprocess on a real empty repo — no amount of mocking can reproduce the exact combination of exit code, stderr text, and empty stdout that git emits in this state. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking git to return empty output would not reproduce a missing HEAD or a non-zero exit code from rev-parse.
behaviors.test_description: Run 'git init' in a temporary directory (no commits), then invoke the tool as a subprocess pointing at that directory. Assert: exit code is 0, stderr is empty (or contains no stack trace), and the output report contains zero counts across all categories.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: Verifying count accuracy requires a ground-truth git repository with a known, controlled history. The failure modes include off-by-one errors, double-counting merge commits, missing files across commit ranges, or misreading git log --stat output. None of these are catchable with mocked git output because the mock would encode the same assumptions as the tool. MOCK-HIDE: A mock cannot reveal disagreement between what the tool counts and what git actually recorded — the mock always agrees with whatever the tool sends it.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a real git repository and make a scripted sequence of commits: add 3 files, modify 2 of them, delete 1, rename another — all with exact known counts. Invoke the tool as a subprocess. Assert that each category count in the report exactly matches the expected ground-truth values established by the setup script.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: The staged vs. unstaged distinction is a real git index concept. The tool must invoke different git commands for each case (e.g., 'git diff --cached' for staged vs. 'git diff' for unstaged), and the failure mode is one of these invocations being wrong or missing — something only observable with a real git subprocess. MOCK-MISS: The two code paths (staged/unstaged) must be exercised against a real git index; mocking would collapse both paths to the same mock return value, hiding any divergence. This is a behavioral contract between two real system states, not an internal logic bug.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a real git repository with an initial commit. Make a set of file changes. First, leave the changes unstaged and invoke the tool, capturing output A. Then stage the same changes (git add) and invoke the tool again, capturing output B. Assert A == B for all change-category counts.
